sentences.text: In Plaszow itself, I worked in the sewing room and then the kitchen.
You had to report every time anybody came to inspect the camp.
Being that I spoke German, I reported.
And Muller got to know me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I begged him that I wanted to go to Prokocim or to Plaszow.
And I knew my father was in camp.
And he wouldn't give me permission for that.
He said to me, what kind of a dog I am?
You know already.
And what will come there, you don't know.
So you better stay here.
OK, little choice that I have, I stayed.
[LAUGHS] I had no-- nothing else to do.
We stayed-- Was it-- was it unusual to ask requests of the German--
To speak to him?
No, no.
I, I could talk to him.
He liked me very much.
And he also always said that if one wants to be clean, you can be clean.
Because my hair was never cut off.
I had long braids.
It was like a-- you know, certain camps were for the Red Cross, a showpiece, my hair was the showpiece for Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a young boy in, in Plaszow.
His name was Balsan.
He was the shoeshine boy from SS man Muller-- who was very protective of everybody in, in the camp.
We have had an epidemic of typhoid fever.
He protected everybody.
He-- they-- the-- Muller gave out as a report that they had the flu because otherwise they would have killed us all.
This young boy, Muller-- Balsan, went up to Plaszow [NON-ENGLISH] to bring bread for us.
My father by then was in the other camp.
Every time he went, he tried to give my father a bread.
It was bitter cold.
Poland is very cold, high snow.
And we had no clothing.
So he gave a jacket to my father.
He was very helpful to everybody, wherever he could.
Whenever he knew that Muller is in a very crazy mood, he came into the barracks and told us, just be careful.
Everything should be in order.
Nobody should be lying around.
Even when you're sick, get up and do something because he's crazy.
He's going to kill.
When he got in such a mood-- he had stables.
He had chickens.
He went, and he killed.
He had to see the blood.
He was thirsty for blood.
He was a very good-looking man, but a maniac, a schizophrenic.
You couldn't predict what, what he-- he's going to do in the next five minutes.
He, he needed the killing.
He was, you know, brainwashed to a point where he just couldn't function like a normal human being.
By the way, after the war, he was sentenced to life in prison.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From Plaszow, we were sent up to the [NON-ENGLISH] Plaszow, the main camp.
And there, our stay was a short one.
But they used that stay to [SIGHS] degrade us.
We carried stones from one corner of the field to the other corner.
When we had everything carried over, they told us to carry it back-- or wood, whatever they had.
They didn't know what to do with us.
So they just wanted to destroy us mentally.
One day there, my father, who worked in the stone works, heard that a transport came over from Plaszow.
And he came out to see.
And he saw me.
Unfortunately, to this day-- and my biggest regret, if you can picture that, how I feel about it.
He poked me on my shoulders.
And he said, Gerda, I am your Papa.
I didn't recognize him.
He was swollen from hunger.
[SIGHS] [SOBBING] And my biggest regret is, to this day, that I have hurt him.
That I am his only daughter, and I didn't recognize.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am sure that it hurt him.
But maybe he forgave me.
Because I am alive, and he is not.
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From Plaszow, we were sent--
For five minutes, no.
Only a few minutes because Goeth was driving around in his car.
And, well, through the window, he was shooting everybody.
So you couldn't.
It wasn't a social club that you could stand and talk.
They did-- they were watching us, whatever you were doing, or whatever you talked.
Or-- it, it was impossible.
I, I am terribly sorry that I did that to him, but not knowingly and, and not intentionally.
Just-- that was part of the miseries.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From Plaszow, [SIGHS] we were sent to Skarzysko-Kamienna.
We were in a cattle train for 36 hours.
You couldn't sit down.
There was no place.
We were standing one next to the other, 36 hours, without water, without facilities.
Whoever could, survived it.
And we arrived in Skarzysko.
The camp itself, the factory-- that was an ammunition factory-- was inside the woods.
We walked the whole night.
When we jumped off from the train, many of us, their legs were so swollen that they twisted their ankles.
And we had to give support to those that couldn't walk.
And we arrived the factory, oh, it was twilight and were a lot of light there.
And there was sand in front of it.
And we were terribly afraid to go in there, that it's a crematorium.
But you had no choice.
You went in.
And you had to register.
And to-- they told you, everything you have, you have to give them, all your clothes.
And then you go into showers.
You walked into one door, and you stood naked there.
And on the other side, they gave you some clothes full of blood that wasn't yours.
It doesn't matter because that was clothes that they have killed already people before.
So they gave it to you.
But as I walked in into that tremendous room, I saw a lot of women.
And there was a transport that came from another camp.
There were 2,000 women.
Among them was my mother.
And I found my mother there.
And, I don't know, at that age, it was difficult for anybody to understand that I was that lucky to find my mother.
And I took over, like she would be my child and I was the mother.
Wherever we-- you had to stand in the morning at 5 o'clock at an Appell.
So I used to pinch her cheeks, that she should have a little color, that they shouldn't call her out.
As a matter of fact, I brought her into the camp from Cracow.
Also, I went to the Germans.
And I begged them they should give her permission, that she is a young woman-- she was 35 years old-- that she's still capable of working, that they should let her stay in camp.
And they gave me that permission, and she stayed.
But then we were separated.
Here, I found her again.
And I took over.
So every time there was an Appell, they told me-- told my mother, oh, you step back.
You are here with your daughter.
Because they knew me.
I spoke to them.
And as young as I was, maybe I was too stupid to understand what is at stake.
So I spoke up, and I spoke.
Was that your personality before the war?
Yeah, yeah.
I was very popular when we came to Bielitz because every mother wanted their child to be my friend.
I had that-- how, how should I tell you-- that large-city [POINTS] [NON-ENGLISH].
You know?
I was capable, I was, I don't know how to explain that.
But--
No.
No, I, I wasn't beautiful.
I still am not beautiful.
But I had a certain-- at that time, I had a certain finesse that the mothers wanted me to be friends with their children.
No.
With the Nazis, I think I was at that stage that I figured I have not much to lose, and I might just as well fight for what I want.
Because when they tried to liquidate during the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Warsaw Ghetto was not too far from Skarzysko.
And when they tried to liquidate the ghetto, right, they wanted to burn [? all their ?] documents in, in Skarzysko, they called me.
I should-- I sort the papers.
And I was standing out on the railroads and burning all their papers.
All my friends, when they saw me there, they said, oh, poor Gerda.
Gerda will never come back.
Because they, they knew that when you do something for them, they won't let you live.
But in Skarzysko itself, I worked in an ammunition factory.
And one-- I came for the night shift, which was 7:00 in the evening till 7:00 in the morning.
And one of the supervisors came in, stood there, and looked around.
And he called me out.
And I went over to him.
And I asked him, what did I do that you're calling me?
And he started to call me 'you Jewish whore,' you this, you that.
What do you think, I'm going to risk my life for you?
Just to be obnoxious, because I was working.
I didn't do anything.
I was a little girl, I was, at that time, 16 years old.
And he called me into the office where there were six of Germans standing there.
He pulled out a chair and said, lie down and count.
And with a rubber whip with wire inside, they were whipping me, that my body was black and blue.
My skin was away from the bones.
And I counted till 25.
And there was somebody by the name of Master [?
Stein, ?] a German.
And he said, I think she has enough.
She's not going to cry.
And I got up.
And I said goodbye, and I walked out.
And I had to go back to the factory to work through the night.
But I couldn't sit.
I was swollen.
And I was-- it was horrible.
It, it-- like, you can't imagine how that looked.
Nothing, just for the fun of it.
At other times, yeah.
A Polish worker who was working for the underground passed behind the table where I was working.
And he said to me, don't cry, it won't last forever.
And that was, at that point, I broke down, and I cried.
I couldn't hold it back anymore.
Right.
He wanted to console me.
And from then on, any time he could, he left a piece of bread for me on the window.
He couldn't give it to me because then they would have shoot him-- him and me.
But he left it on the windowsill.
And he showed me that I should know I should go and get it.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was so ashamed after the beating that I have committed a crime that when we had to go to the showers, I was standing, facing the wall that, God forbid, nobody should see me that I'm so black and blue.
Because you had to get undressed.
You had no, no choice.
It was-- it-- for months and years, I-- to this day, I feel my back.
[BACKROUND NOISE] I have one side of my leg, between the knee and the hip, absolutely no feeling.
You can-- I went once to a doctor.
And he took needles, and he scratched me.
And I didn't feel it.
And he told me I'm lying.
It was a German doctor.
And then when I got up, I was bleeding all over.
And he did not believe me that I don't feel it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was already after the war.
This was in the United States of America, where a German doctor was working for the German government in order not to pay restitution.
They figured I'm a child.
I, I had every reason to claim restitution.
I was born in Germany.
I lived in Germany.
They-- that I had missed schooling.
I spent three and a half years in a concentration camp.
They figured they're better off to fight me.
I didn't let them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
That was 1944, beginning of '44.
We were shipped to Czestochowa-- to Czestochowa, another concentration camp.
I was still with my mother.
There I also worked in an ammunition factory.
So did my mother.
My mother was so skinny that-- every time you walked into the factory, they checked you up and down whether you are not carrying out ammunition for the underground.
Her ribs were sticking out to such a point we had an Ukrainian guard there.
Every time, he beat her that she's carrying out ammunition.
Her blood was coming from her nose and from her face because he thought she's stealing ammunition.
In the meantime, she was starving to death.
Because many times, I couldn't eat the soup that was made from frozen potatoes.
They are jellied, black.
It was sweet from those frozen potatoes.
I just couldn't.
So she had that one slice of bread.
She gave it to me I should be able to eat it.
And my mother never, in all the years that she was in camp, could swallow a piece of meat if you found it in the soup.
Because that meat was not kosher.
If meat, that was a piece of horse meat-- but if it ever found in the soup.
The funny part was that when you lined up for the soup-- the lines were very long-- we used to ask what kind of soup it is.
If it is cabbage soup, you try to run first, you know, because cabbage comes up.
So you should get a little cabbage.
If it's potato soup, then you went back later because you-- or bean soup, or anything-- because that goes down to the bottom.
So you waited, that this watery part should be given away, that you should have anything.
I mean, we were joking.
We were kidding.
Because otherwise, we couldn't have survived.
For example, there was one girl, we called her the Red Elsa.
She was from Warsaw, very intelligent girl.
She had two containers, so she used to come for the soup.
She said, for Elsa and for me.
She figured she will get away with it.
Many times she got away with it.
And she got two soups, which was-- you could give somebody a diamond, it wouldn't have been as precious as that extra soup.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] All right.
That was when we were Czestochowa.
And we worked hard.
And we had no food.
And we were, by that point, already giving up.
It was three and a half years.
And you cannot imagine.
The winters were cold, and you had no shoes and no clothing.
A rag that I cleaned the machine I stole, and I wrapped my feet into it.
People had only wooden shoes by then because they wouldn't give you leather shoes.
So they had those step-ins like the Dutch clogs.
And, and I-- and that's how we lived.
Sometimes a, a Polish worker would tell you something, that Stalingrad fell, or this is going on.
They told us the Warsaw Ghetto burned, and, and there is no more ghetto.
And the Jews are all killed.
So we knew that we are in a big predicament.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
In 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had birthday on the 15th of January.
And my mother said, Gerda, I promise you that one day, you will still wear a suit made by a tailor.
I said, Mommy, you're dreaming.
You're really dreaming.
I mean, how can you say it to me?
I don't have a pair of shoes on my feet.
And you're promising me-- OK.
On the 16th of January, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we ran away-- a group of 11 people.
Everything was burning around us.
The German hospital-- the screams were going up to heaven.
The bombs.
The Russians were, were bombing already.
And the Germans.
And they were fighting.
And a group of us, 11 friends of mine, we said-- by eleven o' clock everybody was strung.
The Germans didn't know whether they're coming or going.
The warehouse was robbed already.
It was impossible.
So we said, you know what?
Let's get out.
We tried to run away.
And at 11 o'clock at night, we went to the gate.
And we told the ward, just let us go.
And you disappear.
And being that he was scared too, he let us out.
We didn't know where to go, so as we came out, we met some Germans.
And we asked them, in which direction are you going?
So they said, come this way because here still are the Germans.
Naturally that was our best clue.
We went in the other direction.
We were hiding all night long in houses, where we heard only German people in the bunkers, hiding.
Because the bombs were flying.
And you saw more dead people on horses in the street.
You were stepping over dead bodies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at 5 o'clock that morning-- or 1 o'clock in the morning, they took 5,000 inmates, and they shipped them to Germany on that so-called death march.
And not one of them came back.
But we were already on the outside of the camp.
At 6:00 in the morning, somebody came and said, I am from Czestochowa.
I'm going into the city.
When I come back, I'll tell you what the situation is.
If I don't come back, hide in the woods.
So we were waiting.
And by around 9 o'clock, he came.
And he told us that the first Russian tank just moved in.
I tell you, all the years I promised myself revenge.
I will-- whenever I will be able to, if I only survive, I will kill them!
I will do, I will-- I have to revenge what, what has happened to us.
When I got out, I was so meek and so scared and so-- I couldn't think straight.
Fair.
Skinny, but fair.
Look, when you are 16, 17 years old, you cope with things like this better than with anything else.
I was hungry.
So I went into a German warehouse.
And I stole 11 blankets, a roll of blankets from the warehouse.
I schlepped it to, to the place where we-- where-- we didn't have where to go, what to do.
But that group of 11, we stayed together.
And then we traded a blanket for bread, or a blanket for butter or milk.
And, and that's how it started to calm down, the war.
The war was still going till April.
You couldn't move, and you couldn't do anything.
So slowly, we, we tried to get back to Cracow in order to get to Bielitz to see if we find anybody.
By that time, they started to have lists in, in the community centers of people that survived, of people that are looking for others.
Unfortunately, I wasn't that lucky.
I did not find-- not my father, not my brother, not my grandparents, my aunts, my uncles, my cousins, none.
My mother had nine sisters and brothers.
My father had 11.
I couldn't find anybody.
No.
My father-- I was told later on that in November '44, he was killed in Auschwitz.
That was four weeks before the end of the war, after being in the camp for three and a half years.
It-- but the way he lived, the way he was, a gentleman from A to Z, that's how he was in camp, too.
People that were with him said that, such a gentleman.
If there was anything that had to be done and others didn't want to do it, he went to do it just so that there should be no commotion.
He was a very special human being.
And that's it.
And then when we came-- slowly we were in Katowice.
We were in Cracow.
We couldn't find anybody.
We could-- We didn't have anything.
We went back to Bielitz.
The fighting was over, yes.
And in Bielitz, they would not let me into our house, into our apartment.
I was by the door.
And I only saw-- because they never painted, but they removed the pictures from the walls.
So you could see the outline of where the family pictures or whatever pictures were hanging there.
But they wouldn't let us in.
Then I went back to--
--Wieliczka.
Nothing, nothing, nothing.
The only thing I found was a pair of pajama pants that belonged to my father.
And that I have.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to Wieliczka and went to the Polish family, that they should give us what we left with them.
And she told me, what are you-- you don't need it.
My father had a fur coat with fur collar.
She said, oh, I-- when your father will come back, I give it to you back.
That I didn't have a shirt to my name didn't bother her.
And then I saw dresses of my mother.
Oh, I remodeled them for myself.
What do you need it for?
But then I saw a suitcase standing on a closet.
And I said, this is our suitcase.
She says, don't touch it, don't-- that's too heavy for you.
My husband will come.
He will take it down.
I said, nothing is too heavy for me.
I took it down.
There were tablecloths that belonged to my mother with the initials, bedding that had all embroidery and initials.
That was the only thing that I found that I took back.
That's all.
The rest they wouldn't give us back.
Then we were [SIGHS] slowly getting into Bielitz.
And in the same building that we tried to stay was a group of-- it used to belong to the family [?
Rapaport. ?] That was a villa.
And everybody that came back were single people, right?
So everybody shared that same villa.
And we stayed all together.
There was four girls and one girl from Lodz.
And one from-- from, from whatever you could find.
Anybody that came back had a center there.
And we lived all together for a little while.
At that point, I heard that there is an organization called [NON-ENGLISH] from Switzerland.
And the lady that was running it was [NON-ENGLISH].
So I got in touch with them because I had promised myself that if I survived the war I will do something without pay, and I will help others.
So I spent a lot of time in Katowice.
Not that I could afford it, but that was a promise.
And I made false visas in the Czech Consulate in order to get Jews out of Poland-- Russian Jews that didn't have papers, to get them out.
I took out an orphanage in Zakopane where there were 70 children that I took over the Czech border.
And they all went to Aix-les-Bains in France, which most of them went to Israel.
And thanks God they are there.
